items.mistake: Monitoring training loss instead of validation loss for early stopping
items.fix: Monitor a validation metric (val/loss, val/f1, val/rmse) each epoch — training loss can fall while generalization degrades.
items.why: Training loss measures fit on seen data, not generalization — stopping on it misses the overfitting signal entirely.

items.fix: Enable restore_best_weights=True (Keras), load best state_dict (PyTorch), or predict with best_iteration (XGBoost).
items.why: The stop epoch is typically several passes after the best validation epoch — final weights are often overfit.
items.example: Val AUC peaks at epoch 42; stop at epoch 52. Without restore, deployed AUC is 0.79 vs 0.84 at epoch 42.

items.mistake: Using the test set for early stopping
items.fix: Monitor only the validation split; keep the test set locked for final sign-off after checkpoint selection.
items.why: Repeated test-set evaluation during training leaks information into model selection — same problem as tuning on test metrics.
items.example: Wrong: eval_set=[(X_test, y_test)] for early stopping. Right: eval_set=[(X_val, y_val)]; test scored once at release.

items.mistake: Setting patience too low on a noisy validation curve
items.fix: Increase patience, add min_delta, enlarge the validation set, or smooth metrics — avoid stopping on random val fluctuations.
items.why: Small validation sets produce high-variance curves — patience=2 may halt during a temporary dip before validation would improve again.
items.example: 80-row val set with patience=3 stops at epoch 8; patience=15 reaches epoch 24 where val/f1 truly peaks.
